romania.title: O pisică rătăcită pe șinele metroului din București a dus la o mobilizare extraordinară. A fost salvată după zile de căutări
romania.summary: O pisică rătăcită timp de câteva zile pe șinele de metrou din București a stârnit o mulțime de reacții pe rețelele de socializare și a reușit să mobilizeze mai mulți oameni care i-au sărit în ajutor. După câteva tentative de salvare eșuate, felina a fost salvată.

medical.title: 'Polypill' for heart failure cuts hospitalizations and ER visits by 60% in trial
medical.summary: A "polypill" combining three medications recommended to treat heart failure into a single daily dose proved far more effective for patients than taking the drugs separately, a randomized clinical trial led by UT Southwestern Medical Center researchers showed. The findings, published in Nature Medicine, could lead to new ways to treat patients with conditions that require taking multiple daily medications to achieve optimal outcomes.

medical.title: Most breast imaging facilities in both high- and low-deprivation areas offer digital breast tomosynthesis
medical.summary: New research has found that digital breast tomosynthesis (DBT) availability at breast imaging facilities is similar across low- and high-deprivation areas, but facilities in low-deprivation areas are significantly more likely to offer weekend appointments, which may affect access for traditionally underserved populations. The investigative team highlights that access can be influenced by service-time deserts in addition to traditional equipment deserts. The findings are published in the Journal of the American College of Radiology's (JACR) July issue.

medical.title: Placenta as property: Why every mother deserves the right to choose
medical.summary: In many cultures, the placenta is more than tissue left over after birth. It may be understood as spiritually linked to the baby, treated as a companion or sibling, and buried to protect the child or connect them to family and land.

medical.title: Inhaled analgesia is as effective as morphine for early pain relief, study shows
medical.summary: Treating pain while in the field can be very demanding. Researchers have finally identified an effective alternative for providing pain relief during the earliest phase of prehospital care, when establishing intravenous access can be difficult. The study is published in The Lancet journal.

medical.title: Parkinson's patients undergoing deep brain stimulation show little to no cortical Lewy pathology
medical.summary: Researchers at the Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai have found that patients with Parkinson's disease undergoing deep brain stimulation (DBS) show little to no Lewy pathology in the prefrontal cortex at the time of their DBS surgery, despite often having longstanding, clinically advanced disease. Published in npj Parkinson's Disease, the findings provide important new insight into the relationship between Parkinson's disease clinical symptoms and neuropathological progression.

medical.title: Ultra-processed foods linked to brain differences in young children
medical.summary: More than half the calories consumed by young kids in the U.S. come from ultra-processed foods.

medical.title: COVID's lingering shadow faded after omicron—but not for everyone
medical.summary: Six years after the world first learned of COVID-19, the pandemic has faded into an unpleasant memory for many. For others, however, it never fully ended. A long-term study by Hiroshima University has found that while lingering symptoms became far less common after the omicron variant arrived, some people have continued to experience health problems years after infection. The results were published in PLOS One on May 8.

medical.title: 'Drunk riding' behind almost half of fatal electric scooter crashes in Sweden
medical.summary: In almost half of all fatal electric scooter crashes in Sweden, the rider has been under the influence of alcohol. These fatal crashes occur mainly in the evenings or at night, and in all cases, no helmet was worn. This is shown by a new study from Chalmers University of Technology in Sweden and the Swedish Transport Administration. The study also states that most fatal crashes occur with private electric scooters, rather than rentals.

medical.title: Dementia-causing substance turns into a therapeutic 'switch' with new Alzheimer's drug strategy
medical.summary: A substance that worsens dementia has become a "switch" that initiates treatment. KAIST researchers have developed a new therapeutic approach that uses hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂), a reactive oxygen species that damages cells and increases in the brains of patients with Alzheimer's disease, to activate a drug selectively in diseased brain tissue. The team also confirmed improvements in cognitive function through animal experiments, presenting a new possibility for next-generation dementia treatment.

science.title: Space startup to launch India's first private orbital rocket
science.summary: Indian startup Skyroot Aerospace said Thursday it would hold the maiden test flight of the country's first privately developed orbital-class rocket in the coming weeks.

science.title: El Niño is shaping up for a hot summer—could recycled water be part of the solution?
science.summary: With El Niño officially declared for summer 2026, Dr. Laura Fernandez and researchers at Macquarie University are testing the use of recycled water to irrigate trees, helping cool Western Sydney.

science.title: Hidden role of garnet reveals how Earth's 660-km seismic boundary forms
science.summary: Nearly 660 kilometers (410 miles) beneath Earth's surface lies one of the planet's most important internal boundaries. Known as the 660-km seismic discontinuity, it separates the mantle transition zone from the lower mantle and plays a central role in controlling how heat and materials circulate through Earth's interior. This circulation helps drive mantle convection, plate tectonics, volcanic activity and the long-term evolution of the planet. Although scientists have generally attributed this boundary to the breakdown of the mineral ringwoodite into bridgmanite and ferropericlase, that explanation has struggled to account for the complex structures detected by seismic observations beneath subduction zones and mantle plumes.

science.title: 'Show some gratitude'—how this rhetoric shapes views on immigration, even for migrants
science.summary: New Zealand First leader Winston Peters is known for targeting immigrants with inflammatory comments about their place in their new country. He made headlines last year when he urged immigrants who "come here with their ideas, foreign to our country" to "show some gratitude."

science.title: What makes a star a star? A strange 'in‑between' celestial object is testing astronomers' boundaries
science.summary: A star called TOI-2155 lies around 1,350 light-years (839 trillion miles) from Earth. It is a little bigger, heavier and hotter than the sun, and it is not particularly interesting or unusual in itself.

science.title: Quantum gravity tests may mistake ordinary spacetime for superposition
science.summary: Everything around us, from atoms and molecules to planets and galaxies, is governed by two extraordinarily successful theories of physics: quantum mechanics and gravity. Quantum mechanics explains the behavior of the microscopic world, while Einstein's theory of gravity describes the motion of stars, black holes and the expansion of the universe. Yet despite their successes, physicists are still searching for a theory of "quantum gravity" that would unite them into a single description of nature.

science.title: These glaciers are becoming critical climate havens as America's iconic mountain glaciers and their water diminish
science.summary: If you have ever hiked in the high peaks of Colorado, the Wasatch Range in Utah or the Tetons in Wyoming, you've almost certainly seen a rock glacier, perhaps without even knowing it.

science.title: Austin neighborhood tap water tests uncover lead and arsenic in homes
science.summary: For more than a decade, residents of Austin's Colony, a neighborhood in an unincorporated area of southeast Austin outside the city service area, have voiced concern about their tap water's intermittent discoloration and extreme hardness.

science.title: Comet from another star has a composition unlike anything else in our solar system
science.summary: Astronomers have revealed new details about the makeup and age of a visiting comet that was born around a distant star. They conclude that the composition of 3I/Atlas is strikingly different from any object found in our solar system.

science.title: Last-minute launch problem delays satellite rescue mission for NASA
science.summary: A rush rescue mission to save a NASA space telescope remains grounded, this time because of a last-minute launch problem.

science.title: Image: Mediterranean Sea breaks June surface heat record
science.summary: This image shows the sea surface temperature anomaly detected in the Mediterranean Sea on June 29, 2026, compared with the average for the period 1991–2020, with dark red indicating temperatures that exceed the average by up to 8°C (46.4°F).

science.title: How much do friends influence teens' mental health? What a new study can (and can't) tell us
science.summary: During adolescence, young people become especially sensitive to peer influence—more so than at any other time in life. So how does this affect their mental health?

science.title: More colorful songbirds face higher extinction risk
science.summary: In the humid jungle of Vietnam, Natalia Ocampo-Peñuela and Monte Neate-Clegg spent hours patiently waiting to spot the rare "Halloween bird." Officially known as the collared laughingthrush, this songbird has striking orange, silver and black coloring and a distinct, singsong call. It's extremely valuable and heavily traded as a pet in Vietnam, and is considered endangered by the International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN).

science.title: Mission documents ecosystem interactions of radioactive waste dumped in the Atlantic between 1950 and 1990
science.summary: Between 1950 and 1990, more than 200,000 barrels filled with radioactive waste were dumped in the depths of the North-East Atlantic. Following an initial mission carried out between June 15 and July 11, 2025, to map the area where the barrels were dumped, the Nodssum project, led by the CNRS, set sail again at the end of May 2026. The aim was to explore the areas of interest identified during the first campaign.

science.title: Good Morning, Earth!
science.summary: NASA astronaut Chris Williams took this photo of an orbital sunrise from the International Space Station on June 26, 2026. In 24 hours, the space station makes 16 orbits of Earth, traveling through 16 sunrises and sunsets. Learn more about the orbiting laboratory. Image credit: NASA/Chris Williams

science.title: NASA’s Webb Reveals Stars Sparking to Life in Cosmic Celebration
science.summary: NASA’s James Webb Space Telescope has captured the infrared light of numerous features that previously were impossible to see beyond the thick dust of the FS Tau star system. In addition to myriad background galaxies that burst into view like fireworks for the United States’ 250th anniversary celebrations, this image flickers with a number of […]

environment.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
environment.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

environment.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
environment.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

environment.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
environment.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

environment.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
environment.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

environment.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
environment.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

environment.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
environment.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

environment.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
environment.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

environment.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
environment.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

mix_items.title: CSR Nest a finalizat montarea sistemului de retenție a deșeurilor plutitoare la Barajul Leșu

Evenimentul organizat de Fundația Comunitară Galați a adunat ambasadori a zeci de cauze pentru care alergătorii participanți au făcut tot posibilul să ajungă la finish. Pe lângă emoțiile și încurajările care au condimentat cursele din cadrul maratonului, pentru Fundația Motivation evenimentul a arătat așa: 14 utilizatori de scaun rulant... mix_items.title: 'Polypill' for heart failure cuts hospitalizations and ER visits by 60% in trial
mix_items.summary: A "polypill" combining three medications recommended to treat heart failure into a single daily dose proved far more effective for patients than taking the drugs separately, a randomized clinical trial led by UT Southwestern Medical Center researchers showed. The findings, published in Nature Medicine, could lead to new ways to treat patients with conditions that require taking multiple daily medications to achieve optimal outcomes.

mix_items.title: Space startup to launch India's first private orbital rocket
mix_items.summary: Indian startup Skyroot Aerospace said Thursday it would hold the maiden test flight of the country's first privately developed orbital-class rocket in the coming weeks.

mix_items.title: Most breast imaging facilities in both high- and low-deprivation areas offer digital breast tomosynthesis
mix_items.summary: New research has found that digital breast tomosynthesis (DBT) availability at breast imaging facilities is similar across low- and high-deprivation areas, but facilities in low-deprivation areas are significantly more likely to offer weekend appointments, which may affect access for traditionally underserved populations. The investigative team highlights that access can be influenced by service-time deserts in addition to traditional equipment deserts. The findings are published in the Journal of the American College of Radiology's (JACR) July issue.

mix_items.title: El Niño is shaping up for a hot summer—could recycled water be part of the solution?
mix_items.summary: With El Niño officially declared for summer 2026, Dr. Laura Fernandez and researchers at Macquarie University are testing the use of recycled water to irrigate trees, helping cool Western Sydney.

mix_items.summary: Ionuț, elev în clasa a XI-a la un liceu din Municipiul Calafat, pasionat de muzică, cu un glas folcloric aparte,... Articolul Gândirea civică – concertul unei generații: povestea lui Ionuț, un tânăr în fața provocărilor cu care se confruntă tinerii de astăzi apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Placenta as property: Why every mother deserves the right to choose
mix_items.summary: In many cultures, the placenta is more than tissue left over after birth. It may be understood as spiritually linked to the baby, treated as a companion or sibling, and buried to protect the child or connect them to family and land.

mix_items.title: Hidden role of garnet reveals how Earth's 660-km seismic boundary forms
mix_items.summary: Nearly 660 kilometers (410 miles) beneath Earth's surface lies one of the planet's most important internal boundaries. Known as the 660-km seismic discontinuity, it separates the mantle transition zone from the lower mantle and plays a central role in controlling how heat and materials circulate through Earth's interior. This circulation helps drive mantle convection, plate tectonics, volcanic activity and the long-term evolution of the planet. Although scientists have generally attributed this boundary to the breakdown of the mineral ringwoodite into bridgmanite and ferropericlase, that explanation has struggled to account for the complex structures detected by seismic observations beneath subduction zones and mantle plumes.

mix_items.title: Inhaled analgesia is as effective as morphine for early pain relief, study shows
mix_items.summary: Treating pain while in the field can be very demanding. Researchers have finally identified an effective alternative for providing pain relief during the earliest phase of prehospital care, when establishing intravenous access can be difficult. The study is published in The Lancet journal.

mix_items.title: 'Show some gratitude'—how this rhetoric shapes views on immigration, even for migrants
mix_items.summary: New Zealand First leader Winston Peters is known for targeting immigrants with inflammatory comments about their place in their new country. He made headlines last year when he urged immigrants who "come here with their ideas, foreign to our country" to "show some gratitude."

mix_items.title: What makes a star a star? A strange 'in‑between' celestial object is testing astronomers' boundaries
mix_items.summary: A star called TOI-2155 lies around 1,350 light-years (839 trillion miles) from Earth. It is a little bigger, heavier and hotter than the sun, and it is not particularly interesting or unusual in itself.

mix_items.title: Parkinson's patients undergoing deep brain stimulation show little to no cortical Lewy pathology
mix_items.summary: Researchers at the Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai have found that patients with Parkinson's disease undergoing deep brain stimulation (DBS) show little to no Lewy pathology in the prefrontal cortex at the time of their DBS surgery, despite often having longstanding, clinically advanced disease. Published in npj Parkinson's Disease, the findings provide important new insight into the relationship between Parkinson's disease clinical symptoms and neuropathological progression.

mix_items.title: Quantum gravity tests may mistake ordinary spacetime for superposition
mix_items.summary: Everything around us, from atoms and molecules to planets and galaxies, is governed by two extraordinarily successful theories of physics: quantum mechanics and gravity. Quantum mechanics explains the behavior of the microscopic world, while Einstein's theory of gravity describes the motion of stars, black holes and the expansion of the universe. Yet despite their successes, physicists are still searching for a theory of "quantum gravity" that would unite them into a single description of nature.

mix_items.title: These glaciers are becoming critical climate havens as America's iconic mountain glaciers and their water diminish
mix_items.summary: If you have ever hiked in the high peaks of Colorado, the Wasatch Range in Utah or the Tetons in Wyoming, you've almost certainly seen a rock glacier, perhaps without even knowing it.

mix_items.title: Austin neighborhood tap water tests uncover lead and arsenic in homes
mix_items.summary: For more than a decade, residents of Austin's Colony, a neighborhood in an unincorporated area of southeast Austin outside the city service area, have voiced concern about their tap water's intermittent discoloration and extreme hardness.

mix_items.title: Ultra-processed foods linked to brain differences in young children
mix_items.summary: More than half the calories consumed by young kids in the U.S. come from ultra-processed foods.

mix_items.title: Comet from another star has a composition unlike anything else in our solar system
mix_items.summary: Astronomers have revealed new details about the makeup and age of a visiting comet that was born around a distant star. They conclude that the composition of 3I/Atlas is strikingly different from any object found in our solar system.

mix_items.title: COVID's lingering shadow faded after omicron—but not for everyone
mix_items.summary: Six years after the world first learned of COVID-19, the pandemic has faded into an unpleasant memory for many. For others, however, it never fully ended. A long-term study by Hiroshima University has found that while lingering symptoms became far less common after the omicron variant arrived, some people have continued to experience health problems years after infection. The results were published in PLOS One on May 8.

mix_items.title: 'Drunk riding' behind almost half of fatal electric scooter crashes in Sweden
mix_items.summary: In almost half of all fatal electric scooter crashes in Sweden, the rider has been under the influence of alcohol. These fatal crashes occur mainly in the evenings or at night, and in all cases, no helmet was worn. This is shown by a new study from Chalmers University of Technology in Sweden and the Swedish Transport Administration. The study also states that most fatal crashes occur with private electric scooters, rather than rentals.

mix_items.title: Last-minute launch problem delays satellite rescue mission for NASA
mix_items.summary: A rush rescue mission to save a NASA space telescope remains grounded, this time because of a last-minute launch problem.

mix_items.title: Image: Mediterranean Sea breaks June surface heat record
mix_items.summary: This image shows the sea surface temperature anomaly detected in the Mediterranean Sea on June 29, 2026, compared with the average for the period 1991–2020, with dark red indicating temperatures that exceed the average by up to 8°C (46.4°F).

mix_items.title: How much do friends influence teens' mental health? What a new study can (and can't) tell us
mix_items.summary: During adolescence, young people become especially sensitive to peer influence—more so than at any other time in life. So how does this affect their mental health?

mix_items.title: Dementia-causing substance turns into a therapeutic 'switch' with new Alzheimer's drug strategy
mix_items.summary: A substance that worsens dementia has become a "switch" that initiates treatment. KAIST researchers have developed a new therapeutic approach that uses hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂), a reactive oxygen species that damages cells and increases in the brains of patients with Alzheimer's disease, to activate a drug selectively in diseased brain tissue. The team also confirmed improvements in cognitive function through animal experiments, presenting a new possibility for next-generation dementia treatment.

mix_items.title: More colorful songbirds face higher extinction risk
mix_items.summary: In the humid jungle of Vietnam, Natalia Ocampo-Peñuela and Monte Neate-Clegg spent hours patiently waiting to spot the rare "Halloween bird." Officially known as the collared laughingthrush, this songbird has striking orange, silver and black coloring and a distinct, singsong call. It's extremely valuable and heavily traded as a pet in Vietnam, and is considered endangered by the International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN).

mix_items.title: Mission documents ecosystem interactions of radioactive waste dumped in the Atlantic between 1950 and 1990
mix_items.summary: Between 1950 and 1990, more than 200,000 barrels filled with radioactive waste were dumped in the depths of the North-East Atlantic. Following an initial mission carried out between June 15 and July 11, 2025, to map the area where the barrels were dumped, the Nodssum project, led by the CNRS, set sail again at the end of May 2026. The aim was to explore the areas of interest identified during the first campaign.

mix_items.title: Good Morning, Earth!
mix_items.summary: NASA astronaut Chris Williams took this photo of an orbital sunrise from the International Space Station on June 26, 2026. In 24 hours, the space station makes 16 orbits of Earth, traveling through 16 sunrises and sunsets. Learn more about the orbiting laboratory. Image credit: NASA/Chris Williams

mix_items.title: NASA’s Webb Reveals Stars Sparking to Life in Cosmic Celebration
mix_items.summary: NASA’s James Webb Space Telescope has captured the infrared light of numerous features that previously were impossible to see beyond the thick dust of the FS Tau star system. In addition to myriad background galaxies that burst into view like fireworks for the United States’ 250th anniversary celebrations, this image flickers with a number of […]

mix_items.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
mix_items.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

mix_items.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
mix_items.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

mix_items.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
mix_items.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

mix_items.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
mix_items.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

mix_items.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
mix_items.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

mix_items.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
mix_items.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

mix_items.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
mix_items.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

mix_items.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
mix_items.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

joke_ro.text: M-am apucat de ordine in casa. Casa s-a opus.

satire_ro.title: Charlie Ottley va promova Craiova. Cine e cascadorul care îl dublează în scenele cu bătăi între clanuri
